title: Winning Lottery Tickets in Neural Networks via a Quantum-Inspired Classical Algorithm
authors: Natsuto Isogai, Hayata Yamasaki, Sho Sonoda, Mio Murao
abstract: Quantum machine learning (QML) aims to accelerate machine learning tasks by exploiting quantum computation. Previous work studied a QML algorithm for selecting sparse subnetworks from large shallow neural networks. Instead of directly solving an optimization problem over a large-scale network, this algorithm constructs a sparse subnetwork by sampling hidden nodes from an optimized probability distribution defined using the ridgelet transform. The quantum algorithm performs this sampling in time $O(D)$ in the data dimension $D$, whereas a naive classical implementation relies on handling exponentially many candidate nodes and hence takes $\exp[O(D)]$ time. In this work, we construct and analyze a quantum-inspired fully classical algorithm for the same sampling task. We show that our algorithm runs in time $O(\operatorname{poly}(D))$, thereby removing the exponential dependence on $D$ from the previous classical approach. Numerical simulations show that the proposed sampler achieves empirical risk comparable to exact sampling from the optimized distribution and substantially lower than sampling from the non-optimized uniform distribution, while also exhibiting exponentially improved runtime scaling compared with the conventional classical implementation. These successful dequantization results show that sparse subnetwork selection via optimized sampling can be achieved classically with polynomial data-dimension scaling on conventional computers without quantum hardware, providing an alternative to the existing quantum algorithm.
summary: This paper presents a classical algorithm that performs a task previously thought to require quantum advantage: efficiently sampling optimal hidden nodes for sparse neural networks. By exploiting the mathematical structure of the ridgelet transform, the authors achieve polynomial scaling, providing a powerful classical alternative to quantum machine learning approaches for subnetwork selection.
main_problem: Efficiently identifying sparse, high-performing subnetworks (winning lottery tickets) within large neural networks by overcoming the exponential computational bottleneck of sampling from an optimized probability distribution.
main_result: The authors developed a 'dequantized' classical algorithm that achieves polynomial-time scaling in the data dimension D, effectively replacing a previously proposed quantum algorithm without exponential overhead.
method: The method uses a randomized classical rejection sampling algorithm that exploits the isometry structure of the discrete ridgelet transform to avoid dense matrix inversion.
model_or_system: A discretized single-hidden-layer neural network using a ridgelet-based architecture over a finite field Z_P^D, where the learning task is supervised regression.
key_observables: Empirical risk, runtime scaling with dimension D, and total variation distance between sampled and target distributions.
important_parameters: Data dimension D, number of training samples M, regularization parameter lambda, smoothing parameter Delta, and precision parameter P.
main_assumptions: The efficiency relies on the specific ridgelet structure rather than general sparsity; it assumes parameters are polynomially bounded and uses a discretized domain.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares empirical risk across different dimensions, showing the proposed method tracks exact sampling; Figure 2 demonstrates the reduction from exponential to polynomial runtime scaling.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of sparse subnetwork selection, defines the ridgelet-based mathematical framework, presents the new classical dequantized algorithm and an improved quantum version, provides theoretical proofs for complexity and correctness, and concludes with numerical simulations validating the efficiency and performance.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Empirical risk of sparse subnetworks constructed from N sampled hidden nodes. We compare exact sampling from the optimized distribution (blue solid line), our dequantized classical algorithm with δ = 0.1 (orange dashdot line), δ = 0.7 (green dashed line), and sampling from the non-optimized uniform distribution (red solid line). The experiments use N ∈{8, 16, . . . , 4096}. Curves show the mean over 50 executions, while error bars are the standard error of the mean.

Figure 1 shows that for all tested dimensions, our sampler closely follows the exact sampler from the optimized distribution. This implies that the proposed rejection sampling preserves the useful bias of the optimized distribution. In contrast, sampling from the non-optimized uniform distribution

Figure 2: Runtime comparison between the naive sampling algorithm (blue solid line) and our dequantized classical algorithm (orange dashed line) in Theorem 2. Each curve shows the mean over 20 executions, while error bars are 95% confidence intervals. Fitted curves are scaling guides.
Figure 3: The function fD to be learned and the activation function g, when the dimension D = 1 and P = 7.
Figure 3 shows the function fD and the activation function g for the case D = 1, P = 7.
